title: practical english usage - Part 182
content:
clauses: the tall man who. . .;             237    relatives: advanced points 234    identifying and non-identifying           236    what 233     relatives: basic information       235   whose contents       of  this   section recovered soon. ►    237.17 This is a letter from my  father, whom  we  hope will be fully 0 The  children will have a garden which  to play in. ►    237.14 0 I can't think of anybody whom  to invite. ►    237.14 0 ©     You're the one that know where to go. ►    237  13 ©     The  idea was interesting which  she put forward. ►    237.12 He liked the people with who  he worked.►    237.8 0 We   need a house we  can stay for a few days. ►    237.7 0 He's married to a singer whose you may have heard. ►    235.3 0 ©     We   met a man of whom  I've forgotten the name. ►    235.2 ©     I poured him a glass of wine, he drank at once. ►    234.4 ©     This is Naomi, that sells the tickets. ►    234.3 Q   I lent him ‘ The  Old  Man and the Sea', that is easy to read. ►    234.3 Dorota, who  does my  hair is from Poland. ►    234.2 0 ©     He got married again, what surprised everybody. ►    233.8, 236.2 ©     I saw a girl whose her hair came down to her waist. ►    233.7 I've found the keys that you were looking for them. ►    233.6 o ©     All what you say is true. ►    233.4 ©     Is that all which  is left? ►    233.4 ©     What's the name of the tall man which  just came in? ►    233.1 Do  you   know     what's    wrong     with    these,    and   why?

give extra information ('non-identifying clauses').

what we  are talking about (‘ identifying clauses'), and those that typically just There are two kinds of relative clause (►  234): those that typically say who  or pronouns*. That can also be used as a relative pronoun.

When  who, which, where, etc are used in this way, they are called ‘ relative Leslie, who works with me, often babysits for us.

He lives in a village where there are no shops.

There's a programme tonight which you might like.

Do you know the people who live next door?

more information about them. These are called ‘ relative clauses'.

to modify nouns and some pronouns - to identify people and things, or to give Clauses beginning with question words (e.g. who, which, where) are often used What    are   relative      clauses?

Section 21  Relative Clauses grammar •  233    relatives: basic information in^ɪ-garage:) not He's got a new girlfriend who works in a garage, (     ... who she-works - He's got a new girlfriend. She works in a garage.

one subject or object in a relative clause is enough. Compare: As subjects or objects, who(m), which and that replace words like she, him  or it 6 one   subject      or  object     is  enough This is not possible in all relative clauses: ►    234.

I forget most of the films I see.  All I want is your happiness.

Do you remember the people we met in Italy?

Object pronouns can often be left out.

5 leaving      out   object     pronouns:      the    people        we    met not All that you say is certainly true, (     All what-you soy...) Note that what (►  236) cannot be used in these cases.

It's the best film that's ever been made about madness.

I hope the little that I've done has been useful.

The only thing that matters is to find our way home.

(More natural than . .. anything which ...) Have you got anything that belongs to me?

Is this all that's left? (More natural than ... all which is left?) any(thing), no(thing), none, little, few, much,  only, and after superlatives.

That is especially common after quantifiers like all, euery(thing), something), 4 all   that,     only     ...  that,     etc I forget most of the films that I see.

Do you remember the people that we met in Italy?

I like people that smile a lot. This is the key that opens the garage.

We   often use that instead of who or which, especially in an informal style.

3  that     = who/which I forget most of the films which I see. (Which is the object of see.) Do you remember the people who we met in Italy? (Who is the object of met.) unusual in an informal style (►   626).
